text: The surgeon opens the peritoneum to access the extraperitoneal spaces, making an incision laterally to the parietal part of the broad ligament above the external iliac vessels. The incision extends from the parietal origin of the round ligament to the gonadal vessels at their crossing with the external iliac vessels. The course of both ureters is identified transperitoneally to ensure their protection during further dissection.

The surgeon dissects the psoas muscle along its epimysium, stopping at the deep circumflex vein, which marks the caudal limit of the dissection. The obturator and internal iliac lymph nodes are visible as prevalent sites for metastasis.
The surgeon dissects the lateral pelvic sidewall, extending the dissection of the psoas muscle up to the bifurcation of the common iliac artery, aiming to reach the puborectalis muscle.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the genitofemoral nerve, which is located more medially in this case, ensuring careful dissection around the nerve to avoid damage.

The surgeon performs medial dissection from the surface of the psoas muscle to the lateral side of the external iliac vessels. The block of lymph nodes is pulled medially, creating a space between the lateral side of the external iliac vessels and the psoas and obturator muscles until the obturator nerve is reached. A window is created under the external iliac vessels during deep dissection, preparing the lateral meridian of the lymph node packet for removal.

The surgeon performs medial dissection to separate the medial side of the external iliac vessels from surrounding tissue and lymph nodes. The external iliac vessels are pulled laterally to facilitate dissection of the medial meridian of the lymph nodes packet. A window is created under the iliac vein to access the previously dissected area lateral to the iliac vessels and obturator nerve.

The surgeon carefully dissects the medial side of the external iliac vessels, ensuring the ureter, which is visible distending onto the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, is not injured. Upon completion, the medial and orally dissected lateral meridian of the lymph nodes packet is visibly separated from surrounding structures.

The surgeon dissects the paravesical fossa, identifying the umbilical artery as a key landmark. The dissection continues until the puborectalis muscle is reached at the bottom of the space, making the obturator nerve easily visible.

The surgeon performs dissection in the avascular space using pneumodissection, following the posterior site of the umbilical artery and internal iliac artery, ensuring clear separation along cleavage planes.

The surgeon mobilizes the obturator, internal, and external iliac lymph nodes laterally by dissecting the medial and lateral margins of the lymph node packet in the extraperitoneal space. The anatomical landmarks are identified, but no lymph nodes have been removed yet.

The surgeon sections the distal caudal margin of the lymph node packet, exposing the pectineal ligament and coronamortis veins. The previously dissected obturator nerve is visible and preserved. Hemostasis is achieved by coagulating the lymphatic vessels in the operated area.
The surgeon carefully strips the obturator nerve by gently detaching the surrounding lymph nodes, ensuring the nerve remains intact and visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon sections the proximal margin of the lymph node packet, exposing the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. Occasionally, the middle of the common iliac artery is noted as the cephalic limit of dissection.
The surgeon uses bipolar electrocautery to dissect tissues and lymph nodes, achieving hemostasis and closure of lymphatic vessels, which may reduce lymphocytic formation.
